questions.question:
The distribution of employees at the rank of executives, across different companies C1, C2, ..., C6 is presented in the pie chart given above. The ratio of executives with a management degree to those without a management degree in each of these companies is provided in the table below. The total number of executives across all companies is 10,000.

Company | Ratio
C1      | 3 : 2
C2      | 1 : 4
C3      | 1 : 3
C4      | 2 : 3
C5      | 9 : 1

The pie chart shows C2 = 5% and C5 = 20% of all executives.

What is the number of management degree holders among the executives in companies C2 and C5 together?

A. 225   B. 600   C. 1900   D. 2500
questions.to_find: Total number of management degree holders among executives in C2 and C5 combined

understand.plain: A pie chart distributes 10,000 executives across six companies. A separate table gives the ratio of degree-holders to non-degree-holders in each company. We use the pie chart to find how many executives belong to C2 and C5, then apply each ratio to find how many of those hold management degrees.

keywords.explain: Each slice of the pie represents a company's share of the total; the percentage multiplied by the total gives the count for that company.
keywords.example: C5 occupies 20% of the pie, so C5 has 20% of 10,000 = 2,000 executives.

keywords.explain: A ratio a : b means for every (a + b) people, a have the desired attribute. The fraction with the attribute is a/(a+b).
keywords.example: Ratio 9:1 means 9 out of every 10 executives hold a management degree.

keywords.explain: The subset of executives in a company who possess a management degree, calculated as (degree-holder fraction) x (company executive count).
keywords.example: If a company has 500 executives with ratio 1:4, then 500 x 1/5 = 100 hold management degrees.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 520 220' width='520' height='220'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='520' height='220' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='260' y='28' text-anchor='middle' class='ink lbl'>C2 and C5 — Management Degree Holders</text><rect x='20' y='50' width='220' height='80' rx='8' class='hi'/><text x='130' y='78' text-anchor='middle' class='ink tag'>Company C2 (5%)</text><text x='130' y='100' text-anchor='middle' class='ink sub'>Executives: 5% x 10,000 = 500</text><text x='130' y='120' text-anchor='middle' class='ink sub'>Ratio 1:4 → degree = 500 x 1/5 = 100</text><rect x='280' y='50' width='220' height='80' rx='8' class='hi'/><text x='390' y='78' text-anchor='middle' class='ink tag'>Company C5 (20%)</text><text x='390' y='100' text-anchor='middle' class='ink sub'>Executives: 20% x 10,000 = 2,000</text><text x='390' y='120' text-anchor='middle' class='ink sub'>Ratio 9:1 → degree = 2,000 x 9/10 = 1,800</text><rect x='160' y='155' width='200' height='44' rx='8' style='fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);'/><text x='260' y='183' text-anchor='middle' style='fill:#fff;' class='lbl'>Total = 100 + 1,800 = 1,900</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two labelled boxes show C2 contributing 100 and C5 contributing 1800 management degree holders, summing to 1900.

given.aim: Find the combined count of management degree holders in C2 and C5

terms.meaning: 10,000 executives are spread across all six companies as per the pie chart
terms.connects: Provides the base for computing company-wise head counts

terms.meaning: C2 holds 5% of the total executives
terms.connects: Feeds into ratio calculation for C2 management holders

terms.meaning: C5 holds 20% of the total executives
terms.connects: Feeds into ratio calculation for C5 management holders

terms.meaning: For every 5 executives in C2, exactly 1 holds a management degree
terms.connects: Determines how many of the 500 C2 executives are degree holders

terms.meaning: For every 10 executives in C5, exactly 9 hold a management degree
terms.connects: Determines how many of the 2,000 C5 executives are degree holders

given.plan: Multiply the total executive count by each company's pie chart percentage to get company executive counts, then multiply by the management degree fraction from the ratio table, and finally add the two results.

steps.title: Find executive count in C2 and C5
steps.note: The percentages 5% for C2 and 20% for C5 are read from the pie chart slices.

steps.title: Apply ratio to find degree holders in C2
steps.formula_raw: degree holders = total_company x a/(a+b)  where ratio is a:b
steps.apply: C2 ratio = 1:4, so fraction = 1/5. Degree holders in C2 = 500 x (1/5) = 100.
steps.note: The denominator of the fraction is always (a + b), the total parts in the ratio.

steps.title: Apply ratio to find degree holders in C5
steps.apply: C5 ratio = 9:1, so fraction = 9/10. Degree holders in C5 = 2,000 x (9/10) = 1,800.
steps.note: A high ratio 9:1 means almost all C5 executives are degree holders.

steps.title: Add results for C2 and C5

solution.result: 1,900 executives in C2 and C5 together hold a management degree.

questions.formula_note: Standard ratio fraction and percentage-of-total formulas are used; 'percentage-of-total' and 'ratio-fraction' are new additions to the registry.

questions.question:
A certain country has 506 universities and 25561 colleges. These are categorised into Grades I, II, and III as shown in the given pie charts.

Universities (506):
  Grade I: 49%,  Grade II: 42%,  Grade III: 9%

Colleges (25561):
  Grade I: 68%,  Grade II: 9%,  Grade III: 23%

What is the percentage, correct to one decimal place, of higher education institutions (colleges and universities) that fall into Grade III?

A. 22.7   B. 23.7   C. 15.0   D. 66.8
questions.to_find: Percentage of total higher education institutions (universities + colleges) that are categorised as Grade III

understand.plain: Two pie charts divide universities and colleges separately into three grades. We need the combined percentage of all higher education institutions (both universities and colleges) that belong to Grade III. This requires finding Grade III counts from each pie chart, adding them, then expressing the sum as a percentage of the grand total.

keywords.explain: The subset of universities or colleges classified at Grade III as shown in each pie chart by the relevant sector percentage.
keywords.example: 9% of 506 universities = 45.54, i.e., about 46 Grade III universities.

keywords.explain: When two groups each contribute some members to a category, the overall percentage is (sum of category members from both groups) / (total members of both groups) x 100.
keywords.example: If 46 out of 506 universities and 5879 out of 25561 colleges are Grade III, combined % = (46+5879)/(506+25561) x 100.

keywords.explain: Because universities and colleges differ vastly in count (506 vs 25561), the college figure dominates the combined percentage, so the university percentage contributes very little weight.
keywords.example: 506 universities form only about 2% of the 26067 total institutions.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 540 230' width='540' height='230'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='540' height='230' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='270' y='28' text-anchor='middle' class='ink lbl'>Grade III: Universities + Colleges</text><rect x='15' y='45' width='235' height='100' rx='8' class='hi'/><text x='132' y='70' text-anchor='middle' class='ink tag'>Universities (506 total)</text><text x='132' y='92' text-anchor='middle' class='ink sub'>Grade III = 9%</text><text x='132' y='112' text-anchor='middle' class='ink sub'>= 9% x 506 = 45.54</text><text x='132' y='132' text-anchor='middle' class='mut sub'>≈ 45.54 institutions</text><rect x='290' y='45' width='235' height='100' rx='8' class='hi'/><text x='407' y='70' text-anchor='middle' class='ink tag'>Colleges (25,561 total)</text><text x='407' y='92' text-anchor='middle' class='ink sub'>Grade III = 23%</text><text x='407' y='112' text-anchor='middle' class='ink sub'>= 23% x 25,561 = 5,879.03</text><text x='407' y='132' text-anchor='middle' class='mut sub'>≈ 5,879.03 institutions</text><rect x='120' y='165' width='300' height='50' rx='8' style='fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);'/><text x='270' y='185' text-anchor='middle' style='fill:#fff;' class='sub'>Total Grade III = 5924.57</text><text x='270' y='205' text-anchor='middle' style='fill:#fff;' class='sub'>% = 5924.57 / 26067 x 100 = 22.7%</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two boxes show Grade III counts: 45.54 from universities and 5879 from colleges, totalling 5925 out of 26067, giving 22.7%.

given.aim: Compute the percentage of all higher education institutions (universities + colleges) that belong to Grade III

terms.connects: Contributes to the numerator (Grade III count) and denominator (grand total)
Dominates the combined count due to much larger size

terms.meaning: 9% of universities are classified as Grade III (from universities pie chart)
terms.connects: Provides the university portion of the Grade III numerator

terms.meaning: 23% of colleges are classified as Grade III (from colleges pie chart)
terms.connects: Provides the college portion of the Grade III numerator

terms.meaning: Sum of all universities and colleges: 506 + 25,561 = 26,067
terms.example: This is the denominator for the combined Grade III percentage

given.plan: Find Grade III count from universities using 9% of 506; find Grade III count from colleges using 23% of 25,561; add both Grade III counts; divide by the grand total (26,067) and multiply by 100 to get the percentage, rounding to one decimal place.

steps.title: Compute Grade III universities
steps.note: The 9% figure is read from the universities pie chart segment labelled Grade III.

steps.title: Compute Grade III colleges
steps.note: The 23% figure is read from the colleges pie chart segment labelled Grade III.

steps.title: Total Grade III institutions
steps.note: Colleges dominate because they outnumber universities by about 50:1.

steps.title: Compute combined percentage of Grade III
steps.note: Rounded to one decimal place gives 22.7%, matching option A.

solution.result: 22.7% of all higher education institutions in the country fall into Grade III.

questions.formula_note: Only the basic percentage-of-total and sum formulas are needed; 'sum-rule' exists in the registry while 'percentage-of-total' is a new formula added below.

formulas.name: Percentage of a Total
formulas.plain: count = (p / 100) x total; equivalently, p% = (part / total) x 100

formulas.name: Fraction from a Ratio
formulas.plain: given ratio a : b, the fraction representing part a is a/(a+b)
